moveAnnotations.annotation: We're opening with the queen's pawn, staking our claim to the center and immediately controlling the important e5 and c5 squares. This move is the foundation of many classical openings and gives us excellent piece development prospects, particularly freeing our dark-squared bishop along the a1-h8 diagonal. The d4 opening tends to lead to more positional, strategic battles compared to 1.e4, though it can certainly become sharp and tactical. Our main plan is to build a strong pawn center, develop our pieces harmoniously, and maintain long-term pressure against Black's position.

Black responds with the most flexible and popular defense, developing the knight to its most active square while immediately attacking our d4 pawn. This move is the hallmark of the Indian Defense systems - rather than meeting our central advance with ...d5 or ...e5, Black adopts a hypermodern approach by controlling the center from the sides. The knight on f6 also prepares kingside castling and keeps options open for various pawn structures like ...e6, ...g6, or even ...c5. We need to be prepared for Black's counterplay against our center, as this knight will often support central breaks like ...e5 or ...c5 later in the game.

We're expanding our central control with the English Attack setup, grabbing space on the queenside and supporting our d4 pawn while controlling the crucial b5 and d5 squares. This move defines many Indian Defense variations and gives us excellent chances to build a powerful pawn center, potentially with e4 coming soon. The c4 advance also opens lines for our queen and prepares natural development of our knight to c3, where it will support both central pawns. By playing c4, we're essentially telling Black that we're ready for a complex positional battle where central control and piece activity will determine the outcome of the game.
